_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Vermilion Parish, Louisiana. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses for this Gulf Coast prairie parish. Vermilion was carved from Lafayette Parish in 1844 and anchored by the Bayou Vermilion town of Abbeville. The parish combines Cajun prairie cattle country with Gulf marsh fishing communities and some of the earliest rice cultivation in Louisiana.

current_courthouse.location: Abbeville, LA -- 100 North State Street. Beaux-Arts courthouse on Abbeville's Magdalen Square, facing the town's central plaza.

previous_courthouses.location: Abbeville -- frame courthouse near Magdalen Square. Abbeville was founded in 1843 by Father Antoine Desire Megret, who named it after his hometown in France.
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced after outgrowing the original structure.

previous_courthouses.location: Abbeville -- brick courthouse on Magdalen Square
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by the current Beaux-Arts courthouse. Records transferred intact.

record_losses.cause: Parish creation -- Vermilion was carved from Lafayette Parish in 1844
records_survived: Pre-1844 records remain at Lafayette Parish Clerk of Court (and pre-1823 records at St. Martin Parish, St. Martinville)
record_losses.description: Vermilion Parish was carved from Lafayette Parish in 1844. Pre-1844 records for the Vermilion area are at the Lafayette Parish Clerk of Court. Since Lafayette itself was carved from St. Martin in 1823, records before 1823 are at St. Martinville. For the colonial period, the chain leads back to the Attakapas Post records at St. Martin. This three-courthouse chain (Abbeville → Lafayette → St. Martinville) is the research path for Vermilion families.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Tracing {ancestor_name}'s family in Vermilion Parish requires following the courthouse chain: Abbeville for post-1844, Lafayette for 1823-1844, and St. Martinville for the colonial era -- three courthouses, three towns, one family.
record_losses.source: Brasseaux, Carl A. Acadian to Cajun: Transformation of a People, 1803-1877. Jackson: University Press of Mississippi, 1992.

record_losses.cause: Hurricanes -- Vermilion Parish's Gulf Coast exposure makes it vulnerable to storm damage
records_destroyed: Some loose papers sustained water damage over multiple storms
records_survived: Bound record books survived all major storms
The 1910 courthouse's construction provided better protection than earlier structures
record_losses.description: Vermilion Parish's Gulf Coast exposure has subjected the courthouse to multiple hurricanes. The 1886 hurricane, the 1918 storm, and Hurricane Audrey (1957) all affected the area. Abbeville, located inland along Bayou Vermilion, was somewhat sheltered from the worst Gulf storm surge, but the courthouse sustained roof leaks and water intrusion at various times. Bound record books survived. Hurricane Audrey in 1957 was catastrophic for the coastal communities of Cameron Parish to the west but Abbeville's inland position limited courthouse damage to minor water intrusion.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Vermilion Parish's courthouse records survived the hurricanes, but {ancestor_name}'s family may have been among the coastal fishing families whose personal papers, church records, and community documents were destroyed by storm surge -- particularly if they lived in the Gulf marsh communities south of Abbeville.
record_losses.source: Post, Lauren C. Cajun Sketches from the Prairies of Southwest Louisiana. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1962.

deeds.completeness: Good -- continuous conveyance records from parish creation. Some early conveyances are in French.
deeds.gaps: Pre-1844 conveyances at Lafayette Parish (and pre-1823 at St. Martin Parish).
deeds.repository: Vermilion Parish Clerk of Court, Abbeville

wills_estates.completeness: Good -- succession records from parish creation. The succession inventories for cattle-ranching families are distinctive -- they list livestock brands, cattle herds, and prairie land alongside household goods.
wills_estates.gaps: Pre-1844 successions at Lafayette Parish. The cattle-economy successions are culturally specific to the Cajun prairie.
wills_estates.repository: Vermilion Parish Clerk of Court, Abbeville

marriages.completeness: Good from parish creation. Catholic sacramental records from St. Mary Magdalen Church in Abbeville (est. 1843) parallel the civil records.
marriages.gaps: Pre-1844 civil marriages at Lafayette Parish. Church records at St. Martinville cover the colonial period.
marriages.repository: Vermilion Parish Clerk of Court; Diocese of Lafayette archives
marriages.online_access: Some indexes at Louisiana Secretary of State

court_minutes.completeness: Good -- continuous from parish creation. Police Jury records include cattle brand registrations that are uniquely valuable for the prairie economy.
court_minutes.gaps: Pre-1844 court records at Lafayette Parish.
court_minutes.repository: Vermilion Parish Clerk of Court, Abbeville

vital_records.completeness: Fair -- compliance slow in rural and coastal communities. Church records are more reliable for the early registration period. Coastal fishing communities may have lower registration rates than inland farming communities.
vital_records.repository: Louisiana Office of Vital Records, New Orleans

substitute_sources.source_type: Lafayette and St. Martin Parish records (pre-1844 / pre-1823)
substitute_sources.description: The three-courthouse chain: Vermilion families before 1844 appear in Lafayette Parish records. Before 1823, they appear in St. Martin Parish records at St. Martinville. For the colonial period, the Attakapas Post records at St. Martinville are the foundational source.
substitute_sources.repository: Lafayette Parish Clerk of Court; St. Martin Parish Clerk of Court, St. Martinville
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The three-courthouse chain is the essential research strategy for Vermilion Parish families. Each step back in time leads to a different courthouse in a different town.
substitute_sources.source: Hebert, Donald J. Southwest Louisiana Records. Eunice, LA: self-published, 1974-2000.

substitute_sources.description: St. Mary Magdalen Church in Abbeville (est. 1843) and churches in Kaplan, Gueydan, Erath, and the coastal communities maintain sacramental registers. Church records in the coastal fishing communities may be the only surviving records for families who lived beyond the reach of civil registration.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual parish churches; Diocese of Lafayette archives
substitute_sources.era_coverage: 1843-present (Abbeville); older records at neighboring parish churches
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Catholic records supplement civil records throughout Vermilion Parish. In the coastal fishing villages -- Pecan Island, Intracoastal City, and the cheniere communities -- church records may be the only institutional documentation of family events.

substitute_sources.description: The Police Jury records for Vermilion Parish include cattle brand registrations that document the open-range cattle economy. Each family's brand was a unique identifier, and brand disputes were a significant portion of early court business.
substitute_sources.repository: Vermilion Parish Clerk of Court, Abbeville
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Cattle brands are a distinctive Cajun prairie record type. A brand registration establishes that a family was engaged in the cattle economy and places them in the prairie landscape. Brand inheritance patterns can reveal family connections.
substitute_sources.source: Post, Lauren C. Cajun Sketches from the Prairies of Southwest Louisiana. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1962.

narrative_hooks: Tracing {ancestor_name} in Vermilion Parish means following the courthouse chain -- Abbeville for post-1844, Lafayette for the early 1800s, and St. Martinville for the colonial era, three courthouses that tell one family's story across three generations of parish creation.

The cattle brand registered in {ancestor_name}'s name at the Vermilion courthouse was as individual as a signature -- a mark burned into the hide of every cow on the open prairie, and the family's most important property claim in a landscape with no fences.

If {ancestor_name}'s family lived in the Vermilion coastal marshes, their community records may have been destroyed by hurricanes that left the inland courthouse standing but washed away the fishing villages, the chapels, and the family Bibles.

Father Megret founded Abbeville in 1843, naming it after his French hometown -- and {ancestor_name}'s earliest Vermilion Parish records were created in a town that was one year old, in a parish that did not yet exist.
source_references: Hebert, Donald J. Southwest Louisiana Records. 39 vols. Eunice, LA: self-published, 1974-2000.
Brasseaux, Carl A. Acadian to Cajun: Transformation of a People, 1803-1877. Jackson: University Press of Mississippi, 1992.
Post, Lauren C. Cajun Sketches from the Prairies of Southwest Louisiana. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1962.
Brasseaux, Carl A. The Founding of New Acadia. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1987.
